event.text: # Project Management Plan Update —

MAAP-1

**Document Control:**
**Revision:** 2.0
(Change Control Board Approved — Pending MDA

Notification)
**Effective Date:** [Current Program Review Cycle]
**Classification
:** Program Sensitive — Distribution Limited to Program Leadership

, Corporate Executives, and Designated Customer Representatives
**Change Authority:** Program

Configuration Control Board, with mandatory escalation per Section 4
**Supers
edes:** MAAP-1 Program Management Plan, Revision 1.0 (January 2025 Baseline

)

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose and Authority

This Project Management Plan Update formally documents the program
matic response to a confirmed 6-month delay to the MAAP-1 First
Flight milestone, driven by autonomy software certification issues identified during the DO-178C DAL A
verification and validation campaign for the Autonomous Guidance,
Navigation, and Control (GNC) software stack. This update supersedes schedule,
cost, risk, and procurement baseline content established in the January 2025 Program Management Plan Revision 1.0. All
program personnel, suppliers, and customer liaison functions shall
operate against the revised baseline documented herein until a subsequent

CCB-approved revision is issued.

This document has been prepared in response to a Class I
program change per the MAAP-1 Requirements Baseline Section 12
.1, which defines a Class I change as any modification that
affects the program cost or schedule baseline, KPP delivery commit
ments, or constraint compliance status. The 6-month First Flight slip satisfies all
three criteria and therefore carries mandatory Configuration Control Board action,
MDA notification, and — per the Financial and Contract Management governance
framework Section 12.2 — escalation to the CEO and Board of Directors given
that the schedule variance exceeds 90

days on a major milestone.

### 1.2 Nature and Significance of the Change

The
MAAP-1 program baseline established First Flight at 21 May 2030,
with zero float on the critical path running from FTA-
1 fabrication through First Flight, the 24-month flight
test campaign, Type Certification, production transition, and Initial Operational Capability. The proposed
change delays First Flight by six calendar months to approximately 21 November

2030.

This delay is not an isolated schedule event. It is the
materialization of the program's two highest-rated schedule risks —
RISK-S-001 (First Flight Date Slip) and RISK-CR-001 (Autonomous System
Certification Standards Undefined), both rated Critical at Score 16
— and simultaneously activates RISK-C-001 (NRE
Cost Overrun) and RISK-S-002 (Certification Authority Engagement Delays Type
Certification). The root cause — failure to satisfy FAA certification compliance evidence
requirements for DO-178C DAL A autonomous GNC software — is qualitatively different from a generic
hardware integration delay. Certification deficiencies of this class are historically
iterative, difficult to fully scope in advance, and involve resolution timelines partially

outside the program's direct control because they require certification authority concurrence.

The program's own
financial modeling has pre-quantified the direct cost impact at +$62
million with an additional $8 million in liquidated damages exposure, representing a 147
-basis-point margin reduction that places program margin
at or below the 8.5% mandatory escalation threshold. This single event cons
umes approximately 84% of the program's $74 million management reserve. The six
-month slip propagates without relief across the zero-float critical path to
every downstream milestone: flight test completion, airworthiness certification, first production

aircraft delivery, and LRIP Lot 1 customer deliveries.

The strategic
significance of this change is compounded by the fact that autonomous operation is not
a peripheral capability. It is the program's primary value proposition, the basis for KPP-
07 compliance, the differentiating feature underpinning the
450+ aircraft market demand forecast, and the justification for $
85 million in vertical integration investment in an internal 120
-engineer autonomy software organization. A certification deficiency in this subsystem therefore
carries demand, competitive, and investor confidence implications

that extend beyond the immediate schedule and cost impact.

### 1.3 Document Structure
This update is organized to address all ten required programmatic domains: scope
, schedule, change management, cost, risk, procurement, quality, staffing, and strategic disposition. Each section draws
upon the multi-domain specialist analyses performed across the
MAAP-1 IMS, Risk Register, Program Requirements Baseline, Financial and Contract Management plan, Manufacturing
Ramp Plan, Supply Chain and Procurement Plan, Bill of Materials, Customer Demand
Forecast, Program Overview, and the integrated Change Impact Assessment

.

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Scope Baseline Status

The program scope as defined in the Requirements
Baseline remains intact. The full set of 192 production aircraft across five
variants — MAAP-1F (Firefighting), MAAP-1C (Cargo
), MAAP-1A (Assault), MAAP-1M (MEDEVAC), and MAAP-1 Export
— continues to constitute the production objective. The technical requirements, K
PPs, KSAs, and system performance parameters established
in PRB Revision 1.0 are not changed by this update. The
80% green aircraft commonality target, the DO-178C DAL A/
B software requirement, the autonomy reliability threshold of
≤1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour, and all
PRB constraints including the $21.5M per
-aircraft FRP unit cost cap (C-1) remain in force

.

### 2.2 Scope Implications of the Autonomy Certification Root Cause

While
the formal scope baseline is unchanged, the autonomy software certification issue introduces scope-adjacent

risks that must be actively managed. Specifically:

The PRB establ
ishes that variant-specific mission software applications shall interface with the green
aircraft solely through defined ICDs and shall not require modification
to flight control system software beyond parameter-table changes
. If the certification deficiency driving this delay is determined to require changes that
breach this architectural boundary — for example, if the GNC algorithm
architecture requires redesign rather than additional V&V evidence — the
impact escalates from a schedule matter to a hardware configuration change
requiring CCB action with potential effects on tooling, manufacturing

processes, and production configuration across all variants.

The Chief Engineer
shall conduct a formal scope boundary assessment within 30 days of this update's approval to determine
whether the autonomy software remediation effort is bounded within the existing software architecture or requires interface
control document revision. This assessment shall be delivered to the CCB with a disposition

recommendation.

KPP-07 (Autonomous Operation) is now formally designated RED risk
status. The certification issues that caused the First Flight delay are a direct leading
indicator that KPP-07 Threshold achievement — reduced-
crew certification for MEDEVAC and Cargo variants — is at risk
. Per PRB Section 7.1, this designation requires
an immediate program deviation report to the Milestone Decision Authority.
The Program Director shall issue this report within 15 business days of this update's effective

date.

### 2.3 Variant-Specific Scope Considerations

The autonomy software is a common core
system shared across all variants. The certification deficiency therefore affects all variants in the production program,
not only the initial flight test article. The following variant

-specific scope considerations are elevated by this change:

**MAAP-1C (Cargo,
Fully Autonomous):** This variant carries the most demanding autonomy certification requirement
— zero-pilot autonomous operations as the K
PP-07 Objective milestone. It represents the furthest downstream certification
commitment and faces the longest cumulative delay exposure. Customer commit
ments for MAAP-1C configured deliveries should be reviewed immediately for delivery

clause implications.

**MAAP-1M (MEDEVAC):** The KPP-07 Threshold for
this variant (reduced-crew certification) is now at risk. Any military customer operational

planning tied to reduced-crew MEDEVAC IOC dates must be reassessed.

**MAAP-1
F (Firefighting):** This variant retains optional pilot capability longer due
to regulatory constraints on unpiloted NAS operations, providing
a degree of schedule buffer. However, the variant's operational utility —
particularly 24/7 autonomous wildfire suppression capability — depends on the same autonomy certification that
is currently deficient. CAL FIRE initial
deliveries and wildfire season operational commitments are specifically at
risk given the seasonal criticality of fire operations (peak season May through

October).

**MAAP-1A (Assault):** Formation autonomy applications interface with the green aircraft through ICDs. Depending
on the root cause of the certification deficiency, the boundary integrity of these interfaces should be verified as

part of the Chief Engineer's scope assessment.

### 2.4 Configuration Control Board Actions Required

The following

CCB actions are formally required and shall be initiated within 30 days of this document's effective date:

1. Formal
revision of program schedule milestones (First Flight, IOT&
E entry, qualification, FRP gate) per

Section 3.
2. KPP-07 risk status change documentation

and MDA deviation report initiation.
3. RVT
M-0001 closure date revision for all autonom

y-related requirements.
4. C-1 unit cost constraint achievability assessment under the revised
schedule, given FY25-dollar

real-value erosion.
5. Scope boundary assessment for autonomy software remediation per

Section 2.2.

---

### 3.1 Revised Milestone Baseline

> **Critical Note on IOC:** The baseline I
MS documented IOC at Q1-2030, which is prior to the revised First Flight date of
November 2030. This internal inconsistency, noted in the pre-change analysis
, must be formally resolved through the re-baselining process. IOC is redefined as the earliest
operationally meaningful milestone achievable following Airworthiness Certification, now estimated at Q3-2032

at the earliest under a standard production transition timeline.

### 3.2

Critical Path Status

The critical path float remains zero under the revised baseline. No schedule margin
has been added by this change — the slip has been absorbed
into revised milestone dates without the insertion of additional float. The following

critical path chain governs all planning:

> **
Autonomy Software V&V → TRR → First Flight → Phase 6A Initial
Envelope → Phase 6B Expansion → Phase 6C Performance Validation → Phase 6D Autonomous Systems

Validation → Qualification → Production Transition → PA-1 → LRIP Lot 1**

All
activities on this chain have zero float. Any further delay at

any node propagates fully and immediately to all downstream milestones.

### 3.3 Schedule
Confidence Assessment — The Critical Risk of a Second

Delay

The 6-month delay estimate carries significant uncertainty. Program leadership
must recognize that the estimate of "6 months" to resolve autonomy software certification issues is an
internal planning assumption, not a commitment from the FAA or
from the verification and validation process. The following factors reduce confidence in a clean

6-month resolution:

**Root Cause Classification:** The recovery timeline differs materially based
on the nature of the deficiency. If the issue is a DO-178C process compliance gap
(incomplete requirements traceability, MC/DC coverage
shortfall, tool qualification deficiency), a 3-to-4
-month correction is plausible. If the issue is a functional safety architecture deficiency —
meaning the software does not achieve its allocated reliability requirement of
≤1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour — remediation requires requirements analysis,
design change, re-implementation, and full reverification
of affected requirements, a process that typically requires 6
to 12 months under rigorous DAL A discipline. The root cause has
not been formally classified in the program record as of this update's preparation
; this classification is Priority 1 for the Chief Software Engineer and

Certification Manager.

**Regulatory Timeline Uncertainty:** FAA Special
Conditions negotiations for Level 4 autonomous aircraft are inherently unp
redictable in duration. The timeline is not fully within the program's control. Regulatory authority
concurrence on software certification evidence cannot be compressed by adding

internal resources.

**Perfect Storm Proximity:** The program's own
financial scenario analysis modeled a 12-month autonomous GNC certification
delay scenario at a cost of $95 million. The current
6-month delay is the first step on this trajectory
. If the autonomy certification issues are not fully bounded and
resolved within the delay window, a second slip would be severely damaging — to program
finances, to customer relationships, and to corporate confidence. The probability of this Perfect Storm scenario
must be formally re-assessed and updated in the risk register within

30 days.

**Recommendation:** Before issuing revised delivery commitments to
customers or revised production schedules to suppliers, the Certification Manager shall conduct an
independent certification readiness review of the complete autonomy software stack and deliver
a confidence-rated recovery schedule (P50,
P70, P90 completion dates) to the Program Director within 45 days.
If the P70 completion date exceeds 6 months by more
than 60 days, this update shall be immediately revised with a larger slip and customers
notified accordingly. It is substantially better to issue one complete,

accurate schedule revision than two sequential revisions.

###

3.4 Schedule Recovery Options

The following schedule compression opportunities are available and should be formally evaluated

during the 30-to-90-day mitigation window:

**Option A — Opt
ionally-Piloted Configuration First Flight:** If the Phase 6
flight test program can be initiated in a piloted mode
with a reduced autonomy envelope, the software certification issue may be contained
to the autonomous certification phase rather than delaying First Flight itself
. This is the highest-leverage single mitigation available. The PR
B references this approach in Section 3.5. The Chief Engineer and Certification Manager shall deliver a feas

ibility assessment within 30 days.

**Option B — Parallel Flight Test Sequencing:** The
24-month flight test program has some internal sequencing flexibility.
Accelerating Phase 6A and 6B envelope expansion activities while
autonomous systems validation (Phase 6D) continues in parallel on the ground could
recover 2 to 3 months at an estimated cost of $8

million to $15 million, which is substantially less than the liquidated damages exposure of continued delay.

**Option C — Additional
Flight Test Article Activation:** Activating a second flight test article to parall
elize test phases could recover schedule. This option
should be evaluated on a cost-versus-delivery-penalty basis given
that each additional test article represents significant capital investment.
**Option D — Additional Autonomy Software Certification Resources:** Assess whether the
120-engineer internal autonomy software team can be surge-resourced with DO-178C DA
L A specialists. Note that outsourcing the fix is not recommended — it adds markup
costs and reduces IP control — but contractor augmentation with
software verification specialists is viable and may accelerate V&V evidence

generation.

---

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification and Authority
This change is formally classified as a **Class I Program Change** under PRB Section
12.1, as it materially affects the program cost baseline, the schedule baseline, K

PP delivery commitments, and constraint compliance status. Class I changes require:

- CCB action chaired

by the Program Manager with the Chief Engineer as technical authority
- MDA notification
- Customer

notification per contractual requirements
- Corporate executive escalation per Financial

and Contract Management Section 12.2

No activity reflecting the revised schedule baseline shall
be communicated to external parties — customers, suppliers, or regulatory agencies — prior to formal CC
B approval of this update. All internal planning may proceed against

the revised dates in Section 3.

### 4.2 Mandatory Escalation Actions

The following escal
ation actions are triggered by this change and shall be executed within the timelines specified. These are not discret

ionary.

| Escalation Action | Authority Level | Timeline | Basis |
|---|

---|---|---|
| Program VP notification | Program VP | Immediate (within 48 hours of

CCB approval) | Schedule variance >30 days on critical path |
| CF
O and CEO notification | CFO / CEO | Within 5 business days | Schedule

variance >90 days on major milestone; forecast margin <8.5% |
| Board

of Directors notification | Board | Within 10 business days | Schedule variance >90 days on major milestone per governance framework |
| Customer
notification — schedule impact | Contracts / Legal | Within 15 business days,
or per contract notification clauses | Major milestone slip affecting

delivery commitments |
| MDA deviation report — KPP-07 | Program
Director | Within 15 business days | KPP-07 designated RED; PRB §7.1 requirement

|
| Customer notification — liquidated damages assessment | Contracts / Legal | Within 20 business days | LD exposure confirmation

; contractual duty |
| ITAR customer notification (export
customers) | Export Compliance | Within 10 business days | ITAR licensing terms require State
Department coordination on schedule changes affecting ITAR-controlled deliv

eries |

### 4.3 Change Control Governance During the Recovery Period

Given the critically
low management reserve position following absorption of this change (less
than $12 million remaining against $74 million initial reserve), the Program Manager is

instituting the following enhanced change governance measures effective immediately:

**Enhanced
CCB Threshold:** Any subsequent scope, schedule, or cost change with an
estimated value exceeding $1 million or a schedule impact exceeding 30
days shall be elevated to CCB within 72 hours of identification,

regardless of the normal review cycle schedule.

**Weekly EVM Reporting:** Cost
Performance Index and Schedule Performance Index shall be reported weekly to the Program Director until C
PI and SPI are both above 0.95 for four

consecutive weeks following the revised baseline establishment.

**30-Day Recovery Plan Checkpoint:** A
formal program recovery plan shall be presented to the Program Director, CFO, and CEO within 30 days of this update's effective date. The recovery plan shall include:
root cause classification, P50/P70/P90 recovery schedule, funded mit

igation actions, reserve replenishment request, and customer communication status.

**60
-Day External Review:** Given the magnitude of this change and the near-exhaust
ion of management reserve, the Program Manager shall engage corporate leadership to commission an independent
program assessment at day 60. This assessment shall evaluate the adequacy of the recovery
plan, the realism of the revised schedule, and the status of corporate contingency funding commitment.

### 4.4 Equitable Adjustment Assessment

Contracts and Legal shall immediately open
a formal Equitable Adjustment documentation file. The following questions shall be assessed and documented

within 20 business days:

1. Did the autonomy certification requirements evol
ve post-contract due to regulatory standard changes imposed by the FAA (Special

Conditions scope expansion)?
2. Were any certification requirements imposed
by customer-specified interpretive guidance that was not contract

ually defined at contract award?
3. Are there any customer-caused dimensions
to the certification delay (late requirements, delayed government

-furnished data or approvals)?

If any portion
of the delay is determined to have a customer-caused dimension, an EA claim shall be formally
initiated. The program recovered 66% of customer-caused impacts ($11
million of $18.3 million) on a prior
comparable program through proactive EA pursuit. This discipline

shall be applied here.

---

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1

Revised Cost Baseline

The program cost baseline is revised to incorporate the direct financial impact of the 6-month First
Flight delay. The following table presents the revised cost baseline, consistent with the pre-quantified financial scenario

documented in the Financial and Contract Management plan Section 3.2.

The base scenario of +$62M and -
$8M in liquidated damages was pre-quantified in the program's own financial modeling and represents the floor
of financial exposure. The realistic total exposure range of $78
million to $95 million reflects additional cost drivers not fully captured in the base scenario: additional
DO-178C compliance work and regulatory engagement ($8
M–$15M), test range and chase aircraft contract re-sequencing penalties
($2M–$4M), and key flight test personnel retention costs during

the delay period ($3M–$8M).

### 5.2 Non-Recurring Cost Impact

The Bill of Materials documents
total non-recurring costs of $793.8 million. The 6-month delay specifically impacts the following

NRC line items:

| NRC Line Item | Baseline | Estimated Increment | Revised Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Development | $450M | +$20M – $40M | $

470M – $490M |

M |

M** | **~$839M – $884M** |

This NRC increase
revises the per-aircraft amortized non-recurring cost from the baseline of $7
(low overrun) to $8,838,150 (high overrun) per aircraft across
the 100-unit amortization base. This increase propag
ates to all five variant program unit costs and will be reflected in the next B

OM revision.

### 5.3 Management Reserve Status

This is the

most acute financial governance issue generated by this change. The program's management reserve position is critically compromised:

| Reserve

Metric | Status |
|---|---|
| Original management reserve | $74M (1.5

% of contract value) |
| Reserve consumed by this change (base scenario) | $62M (

84% of total reserve) |
| Remaining reserve (post-change, base scenario) | ~

$12M |
| Recommended P70 reserve (program's own

lessons-learned benchmark) | $125M |
| Reserve adequacy trigger (
20% remaining with >50% program remaining) |

Breached |

With less than $12 million in management reserve remaining against a multi-billion
-dollar development and production program with known open risks
, the program is in a reserve adequacy crisis. A formal Reserve Replenishment Plan
shall be submitted to the CFO within 30 days of this update's effective date. The minimum replenishment target
is $50 million to restore P50 coverage; the recommended
replenishment target to restore P70 coverage is $125 million minus the post-change remaining balance

, approximately $113 million in total reserve commitment.

### 5.4

Margin Impact and Escalation Status

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
|

Baseline program margin | 9.9% (target) |
| Post

-change margin (base scenario, -147 bps) | ~8.4% |
| Mandatory

escalation threshold | 8.5% |
| Escalation status | **TRIGGERED** |

The base-
case margin of approximately 8.4% falls below the 8.5% mandatory escalation threshold defined in the Financial and Contract Management governance
framework. This triggers mandatory escalation from Program VP to CFO to CEO per the framework. If any
additional cost growth materializes — through learning curve
underperformance, supplier cost reduction failures, overhead rate
growth, or an extension of the certification delay beyond 6 months — margin will deterior
ate further and potentially approach breakeven under the documented compound risk scenarios

.

### 5.5 Cash Flow Implications

The delay creates a material cash flow trough. The specific drivers are

:

**Extended Engineering Burn Rate:** 350 peak engineers
at loaded rates continuing for 6 additional months without corresponding
milestone payment triggers represents an estimated $25 million to $35 million in additional cash

outflow before revenue events are reached.

**Milestone
Payment Deferral:** If First Flight is a contract milestone payment trigger
, the 6-month slip defers that cash inflow. The program has
previously experienced DSO peaks of 58 days during development
transition periods, requiring corporate credit line draws.
The Program Finance Director shall model the specific cash flow impact and coordinate

with Treasury on working capital adequacy.

**Capital Investment Carrying Cost
:** The $235 million Phase 1 capital deployment is mid-execution. Deployment of
capital against a schedule that has slipped creates carrying
costs without corresponding production activity. The 2025 capital
spend profile of $68 million will partially shift
into 2026, creating compression against the $52
million LRIP-2 preparation budget planned for that

year.

### 5.6 Program Unit Cost Constraint (C-1)

Risk Assessment

## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality Status Assessment

The autonomy software certification failure represents a quality escape
from the software development process — specifically, a failure to achieve
V&V closure within the planned schedule and to
the required standard of certification evidence. From a quality management
perspective, this constitutes a systemic deficiency in the software verification pipeline that must be diagnosed

, corrected, and validated before any certification re-submission.

### 8.2
Software DAL Compliance — Corrective Action Plan

Requirement

The Quality Management Plan tracks Software DAL compliance as a formal program
metric, defined as the percentage of flight-critical software lines of code
under DO-178C DAL A/B process discipline.
The autonomy software certification failure implies this metric is trending Red or has
been Red for multiple Program Management Review cycles. Per the PRB Section 11
, any metric trending Red for two consecutive PM
R cycles requires a formal corrective action plan presented to the Program Manager, with escalation to the MDA if

unresolved after the subsequent cycle.

The Quality Director shall assess
whether the Software DAL compliance metric has met the two-consecutive-Red threshold and
, if so, shall initiate the formal corrective action plan process within 10 business days.

The corrective action plan shall address:

1. Specific DO-178C compliance gaps identified during V
&V (process, structural coverage, or tool qualification

, and completion dates
4. Re-verification scope and schedule
5. Independent verification of corrective

action effectiveness before re-submission to certification authority

### 8.3 Root Cause Classification

— Quality Perspective

The Quality Director and Chief Software Engineer shall jointly
conduct and document a formal root cause classification within 21 days,

using the following decision framework:

**Category 1 — DO-178C Process
Compliance Gap:** Procedures not followed correctly; inadequate requirements tr
aceability; MC/DC coverage shortfall; tool qualification deficiency. Remediation: Process
corrective action and additional V&V evidence generation. Estimated

timeline: 3–4 months.

**Category 2 — Requirements or Design
Deficiency:** Software does not meet functional requirements; GNC algorithm architecture does
not achieve allocated reliability of ≤1×
10⁻⁷ per flight hour. Remediation: Requirements analysis
, design change, re-implementation, and full re
-verification of affected requirements. Estimated timeline: 6–12 months under DA

L A discipline.

**Category 3 — Verification Environment Inadequacy:** V&V environment (
HIL, SIL, test cases) is insufficient to
generate required certification evidence. Remediation: Test environment correction before

meaningful V&V can proceed. Estimated timeline: 2–4 months.

The remediation cost,
schedule, and management reserve impact differ significantly across these categories. The
reserve replenishment plan (Section 5.3) and revised milestone dates

(Section 3) must be conditioned on the root cause classification outcome.

### 8.4 Pre-Delivery

Autonomous Systems Check — Production Risk

The Quality Management Plan specifies that pre-delivery flight
acceptance includes a mandatory Autonomous Systems Check (15 minutes: engage autonomous
mode, waypoint navigation, DAA system test).
No aircraft may be delivered without passing this check. This creates the highest-
consequence quality risk associated with the proposed delay: if the autonomy software root
cause is not definitively resolved before production configuration release, the deficiency migrates from

the test program to the production acceptance line.

At production
scale — with 18 LRIP Lot 1 units and a ramp to
48 aircraft per year at FRP —
a systematic autonomy software deficiency generating production nonconformances would halt deliveries and produce cost and
schedule consequences that dwarf the current 6-month slip. The Quality Director shall establish

and enforce the following gate:

**No Production Configuration Release:**
The autonomy software production build shall not be released for production configuration
until full DO-178C DAL A certification closure is achieved and independently verified. This gate is
absolute and non-waiverable. Any pressure to release a partially
-certified production build to maintain delivery commitments shall be escal

ated immediately to the Program Director and Chief Engineer.

### 8.5 Safety Case Integrity

— FMEA and Hazard Analysis

Any software architecture changes required
to resolve the certification issue must be processed through the FMEA and System Safety Assessment
process per ARP4761. The autonom
y GNC system has been allocated safety-critical reliability requirements
(≤1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour threshold, ≤1×10⁻⁸
objective). Any software change affecting a Failure Mode severity
of 9 or 10 (hazardous to catastrophic) requires
mandatory safety assessment regardless of schedule pressure. The Safety Manager shall review
all planned software changes within the corrective action scope and determine
which require formal FMEA and SSA updates before the next planned

certification submission.

### 8.6 Special Process Qualification Continuity

The Quality Management Plan spec
ifies periodic re-qualification requirements for special processes: composite fabrication (
quarterly oven validation and annual operator certification
), NDT (annual personnel certification), and welding (annual qualification). A
6-month production hiatus does not suspend these requirements. The Quality Director shall confirm that
all special process qualifications are maintained on their existing schedules during the delay period and alloc
ate budget accordingly. Failure to maintain qualifications will require re

-qualification at production restart, adding both cost and schedule risk.

mentation should not replace internal expertise but may accelerate evidence

## 10. Overall

Assessment & Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Executive Summary Judgment
This change is a material adverse event for the
MAAP-1 program. The 6-month First Flight delay driven by autonomy software certification issues is not a routine
schedule variance — it is the materialization of the program's two highest
-rated risks at the program's most technically complex and strateg
ically critical subsystem. The financial impact at the base case is $62 million in
direct cost growth and $8 million in liquidated damages, consuming 84
% of the program's management reserve in a single event and placing program
margin at or below the mandatory executive escalation threshold. The realistic financial
exposure, accounting for likely additional certification costs and operational impacts, ranges

from $78 million to $95 million.

The downstream consequences are systemic.
Every milestone from First Flight to Initial Operational Capability shifts by
a minimum of 6 months against a critical path with zero float. Customer
deliveries for all variants are affected. The
demand forecast's central assumption — FAA Type Certification enabling
customer deliveries on the original schedule — is invalidated. The pre-quantified
demand impact of 8 to 12
units lost in the 2027–
2029 window, representing Risk DR-001 converted from probabilistic to realized

, is now a planning baseline reduction, not a contingency.

The near
-exhaustion of management reserve is the single most acute programmatic concern beyond
the technical root cause. With less than $12 million in reserve
remaining against a multi-billion-dollar program with open critical and
high risks across cost, schedule, and certification domains, any subsequent
adverse event — however modest — threatens to push the program to break
even or loss margin territory under the documented compound risk scenarios

.

**This change is not manageable at the program level alone.** It requires immediate
corporate executive engagement, customer notification, a funded recovery plan, and a reserve
replenishment commitment from corporate leadership, all within

30 days.

### 10.2 Five Priority Actions — Week One
The following five actions shall be executed within the first week of this update

's effective date, before any other programmatic activity:

**Priority 1
— Root Cause Classification:** The Chief Software Engineer and Certification
Manager shall provide the Program Director with a preliminary root cause classification (Category 1,
2, or 3 per Section 8.3) within 72 hours.
This classification drives the recovery schedule, cost estimate, and reserve requirement. All

subsequent decisions are conditioned on this finding.

**Priority 2 — Executive Escalation:** The Program VP shall brief
the CFO and CEO within 48 hours. The briefing shall cover the cost impact, margin threshold
breach, management reserve status, and reserve replenishment requirement. Corporate contingency funding

commitment shall be requested at this meeting.

**Priority 3 — Autonomy Software Team
Retention:** The Chief Software Engineer and HR Director shall identify the top 20 critical autonomy software personnel and init
iate retention package discussions within 5 business days. The Program Director shall address the team directly within 5

business days.

**Priority 4 — Optionally-Piloted Configuration Assessment
:** The Chief Engineer shall initiate the feasibility assessment for an optionally-piloted First Flight within 5
business days. This is the highest-leverage single schedule recovery mit

igation available and must be evaluated before the 30-day recovery plan deadline.

**Priority 5
— Supplier Notification:** The Supply Chain Director shall issue formal schedule change notifications to all
Tier 1 suppliers within 10 business days. Capacity reservation negotiations for sole

-source suppliers shall begin immediately.

### 10.3 Thirty-Day Recovery Plan Requirements

The Program Director
shall present a formally documented Program Recovery Plan to the Program VP, CFO, and CEO within 30

days. This plan shall include at minimum:

**Technical Recovery:** Root cause classification and disposition
; certification readiness review scope and timeline; P50
, P70, and P90 schedule completion dates with confidence methodology

; funded mitigation actions and resource plan.

**Schedule
:** Formally re-baselined IMS per Section 3;
confirmation of which downstream milestones are fully cascaded versus candidates for compression

recovery; optionally-piloted First Flight feasibility determination.

**Financial:** Formal
EAC update per Section 5; reserve replenishment plan; cash
flow model for the delay period; C-1 constraint achievability assessment;

EA claim status.

**Customer:** Customer notification status for
all affected contracts; LD exposure quantification; equitable adjustment
assessment status; proactive recovery communication plan by

customer segment.

**Risk:** Refreshed risk register per Section 6; Perfect
Storm scenario probability re-assessment; second-delay probability with

confidence assessment.

**Governance:** All mandatory escalation actions
per Section 4 confirmed as executed; MDA K

PP-07 deviation report status; CCB action log.

### 10.4 Strategic

Disposition Assessment

Beyond the immediate recovery actions, this change requires the

program leadership team to make a candid assessment of three strategic questions:

**Question 1:

Is the 6-Month Delay Estimate Credible?**

Based on the analysis across program
documents, there is meaningful probability that the actual delay exceeds 6 months. The history
of DO-178C DAL A certification for novel autonomous systems is replete with examples of iterative,
extending certification campaigns that exceeded initial estimates. The program must
resist the institutional pressure to plan against an optimistic
6-month assumption if the root cause analysis suggests a longer timeline.
The cost of planning for 6 months and experiencing 9
months is substantially greater than the cost of planning for 9 months and achieving 6
. The independent certification readiness review at 30

days is the critical decision point for refining this estimate.

**Question 2

: Is the Optionally-Piloted Path the Right Near-Term Strategy?**

The demand forecast, requirements
baseline, and risk register all reference an optionally-piloted configuration fallback as the primary conting
ency for autonomy certification delay. If this fallback is executable —
meaning FAA will authorize First Flight and early flight
test in a reduced-autonomy piloted mode while
full autonomous certification continues — it is the most effective single action
available to preserve schedule, protect customer relationships, and reduce
financial exposure. The Chief Engineer's feasibility assessment at 30 days is therefore
the most consequential technical deliverable in the near-

term recovery plan.

**Question 3: What is the Minimum Acceptable Reserve Position?**

With less than $12 million in
management reserve remaining, the program has no meaningful buffer against any subsequent adverse event. The program's
own lessons-learned benchmark recommends a P70 reserve
of $125 million — the current $74 million initial reserve was
already flagged as undersized. Corporate leadership must commit to a reserve replenishment level sufficient
to provide genuine risk coverage for the remainder of development and LRIP.
The minimum defensible replenishment is $50 million for P50
coverage; $100 million or more for P70 coverage. Any commitment below $
50 million leaves the program in a position where a single additional adverse event can breach margin thresholds and

trigger loss territory.

### 10.5 Customer Communication Posture

The customer
communication strategy for this change must balance contractual obligations (mandatory notification
per contract terms), commercial relationships (preserving customer confidence
and LRIP slot commitments), and honest program transparency. The following principles

govern customer communications:

**Early and Proactive:** Customers shall
be notified before public information or industry reporting could
reach them through alternative channels. The worst customer experience is receiving news of a program

delay from a third party.

**Accurate, Not Optimistic:** Customer notifications
shall present the revised schedule as the program's best current estimate, with honest
acknowledgment of the uncertainty range. Customers who receive ov
erly optimistic communications and then experience a second slip suffer comp

ounded damage to confidence.

**Recovery Plan Focused:** Every
customer communication shall be accompanied by a description of the recovery actions being taken. Customers need to know not

just what happened but what the program is doing about it.

**Variant-Specific:** Customer
impacts differ significantly by variant and delivery position. Communications shall
be tailored to the specific impact on each customer's contract and operational requirements, not generic program

-level notifications.

**Wildfire Season Priority:** CAL
FIRE and USFS customers, for whom delivery
timing is operationally tied to the May-through-October fire
season, require special attention. The program shall assess whether any interim arrangements — ground
training deliveries, simulator access, or phased deployment options — can partially

satisfy operational requirements during the delay period.

### 10.6 Path Forward Recommendation

The Program Director,

with endorsement from the Program VP, recommends the following path forward:

**Immediate (0–30 Days):** Execute all
Priority 1 through 5 actions in Section 10.2. Conduct root cause classification.
Issue executive escalation. Execute supplier notifications. Initiate retention actions. Complete opt

ionally-piloted feasibility assessment. Issue customer notifications.

**Near-Term (30–60 Days):** Deliver the Recovery Plan to corporate
leadership. Obtain corporate contingency funding commitment. Complete independent certification readiness review. Issue P50/
P70/P90 schedule to customers. Begin CCB re-baselining process
. Conduct Integrated Baseline Review with updated EAC and resource loading

.

**Medium-Term (60–120 Days):** Complete the IMS re-baseline. Execute supplier capacity
reservation agreements. Publish updated demand forecast and customer delivery commit
ments. Complete EA claim assessment. Begin productive use of delay window per
Section 7.5. Conduct second-delay probability reassessment at 90 days based on technical

progress.

**Decision Gate (Day 45):** The independent
certification readiness review result is the critical program decision gate. If the P70 completion
date for autonomy software certification exceeds the 6-month assumption by more than 60
days, the Program Director shall immediately revise this update with a larger slip
, notify customers, and present to corporate leadership. The discipline to
make this decision accurately and promptly — rather than planning against wishful optim
ism — is the single most important management action available to protect program credibility and financial

performance.

The MAAP-1 program's fundamental value proposition — first-in-class
autonomous heavy-lift rotorcraft certification, with the autonomous capability that
is the basis of the entire market demand forecast — remains intact
. The certification challenge being faced is an expected consequence of attempting
something genuinely unprecedented in aviation. The program's response to this challenge — technical ri
gor, leadership transparency, disciplined governance, and proactive stakeholder management
— will determine whether this delay becomes a recoverable program event or the first
step in a cascading programmatic deterioration. The actions directed
in this update represent the minimum necessary response to ensure the former outcome

.

# Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1

**Document Control:**
**Revision:** 2.0 (Change Control Board Approved — Pending MDA Notification)
**Effective Date:** [Current Program Review Cycle]
**Classification:** Program Sensitive — Distribution Limited to Program Leadership, Corporate Executives, and Designated Customer Representatives
**Change Authority:** Program Configuration Control Board, with mandatory escalation per Section 4
**Supersedes:** MAAP-1 Program Management Plan, Revision 1.0 (January 2025 Baseline)

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose and Authority

This Project Management Plan Update formally documents the programmatic response to a confirmed 6-month delay to the MAAP-1 First Flight milestone, driven by autonomy software certification issues identified during the DO-178C DAL A verification and validation campaign for the Autonomous Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) software stack. This update supersedes schedule, cost, risk, and procurement baseline content established in the January 2025 Program Management Plan Revision 1.0. All program personnel, suppliers, and customer liaison functions shall operate against the revised baseline documented herein until a subsequent CCB-approved revision is issued.

This document has been prepared in response to a Class I program change per the MAAP-1 Requirements Baseline Section 12.1, which defines a Class I change as any modification that affects the program cost or schedule baseline, KPP delivery commitments, or constraint compliance status. The 6-month First Flight slip satisfies all three criteria and therefore carries mandatory Configuration Control Board action, MDA notification, and — per the Financial and Contract Management governance framework Section 12.2 — escalation to the CEO and Board of Directors given that the schedule variance exceeds 90 days on a major milestone.

### 1.2 Nature and Significance of the Change

The MAAP-1 program baseline established First Flight at 21 May 2030, with zero float on the critical path running from FTA-1 fabrication through First Flight, the 24-month flight test campaign, Type Certification, production transition, and Initial Operational Capability. The proposed change delays First Flight by six calendar months to approximately 21 November 2030.

This delay is not an isolated schedule event. It is the materialization of the program's two highest-rated schedule risks — RISK-S-001 (First Flight Date Slip) and RISK-CR-001 (Autonomous System Certification Standards Undefined), both rated Critical at Score 16 — and simultaneously activates RISK-C-001 (NRE Cost Overrun) and RISK-S-002 (Certification Authority Engagement Delays Type Certification). The root cause — failure to satisfy FAA certification compliance evidence requirements for DO-178C DAL A autonomous GNC software — is qualitatively different from a generic hardware integration delay. Certification deficiencies of this class are historically iterative, difficult to fully scope in advance, and involve resolution timelines partially outside the program's direct control because they require certification authority concurrence.

The program's own financial modeling has pre-quantified the direct cost impact at +$62 million with an additional $8 million in liquidated damages exposure, representing a 147-basis-point margin reduction that places program margin at or below the 8.5% mandatory escalation threshold. This single event consumes approximately 84% of the program's $74 million management reserve. The six-month slip propagates without relief across the zero-float critical path to every downstream milestone: flight test completion, airworthiness certification, first production aircraft delivery, and LRIP Lot 1 customer deliveries.

The strategic significance of this change is compounded by the fact that autonomous operation is not a peripheral capability. It is the program's primary value proposition, the basis for KPP-07 compliance, the differentiating feature underpinning the 450+ aircraft market demand forecast, and the justification for $85 million in vertical integration investment in an internal 120-engineer autonomy software organization. A certification deficiency in this subsystem therefore carries demand, competitive, and investor confidence implications that extend beyond the immediate schedule and cost impact.

### 1.3 Document Structure

This update is organized to address all ten required programmatic domains: scope, schedule, change management, cost, risk, procurement, quality, staffing, and strategic disposition. Each section draws upon the multi-domain specialist analyses performed across the MAAP-1 IMS, Risk Register, Program Requirements Baseline, Financial and Contract Management plan, Manufacturing Ramp Plan, Supply Chain and Procurement Plan, Bill of Materials, Customer Demand Forecast, Program Overview, and the integrated Change Impact Assessment.

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Scope Baseline Status

The program scope as defined in the Requirements Baseline remains intact. The full set of 192 production aircraft across five variants — MAAP-1F (Firefighting), MAAP-1C (Cargo), MAAP-1A (Assault), MAAP-1M (MEDEVAC), and MAAP-1 Export — continues to constitute the production objective. The technical requirements, KPPs, KSAs, and system performance parameters established in PRB Revision 1.0 are not changed by this update. The 80% green aircraft commonality target, the DO-178C DAL A/B software requirement, the autonomy reliability threshold of ≤1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour, and all PRB constraints including the $21.5M per-aircraft FRP unit cost cap (C-1) remain in force.

### 2.2 Scope Implications of the Autonomy Certification Root Cause

While the formal scope baseline is unchanged, the autonomy software certification issue introduces scope-adjacent risks that must be actively managed. Specifically:

The PRB establishes that variant-specific mission software applications shall interface with the green aircraft solely through defined ICDs and shall not require modification to flight control system software beyond parameter-table changes. If the certification deficiency driving this delay is determined to require changes that breach this architectural boundary — for example, if the GNC algorithm architecture requires redesign rather than additional V&V evidence — the impact escalates from a schedule matter to a hardware configuration change requiring CCB action with potential effects on tooling, manufacturing processes, and production configuration across all variants.

The Chief Engineer shall conduct a formal scope boundary assessment within 30 days of this update's approval to determine whether the autonomy software remediation effort is bounded within the existing software architecture or requires interface control document revision. This assessment shall be delivered to the CCB with a disposition recommendation.

KPP-07 (Autonomous Operation) is now formally designated RED risk status. The certification issues that caused the First Flight delay are a direct leading indicator that KPP-07 Threshold achievement — reduced-crew certification for MEDEVAC and Cargo variants — is at risk. Per PRB Section 7.1, this designation requires an immediate program deviation report to the Milestone Decision Authority. The Program Director shall issue this report within 15 business days of this update's effective date.

### 2.3 Variant-Specific Scope Considerations

The autonomy software is a common core system shared across all variants. The certification deficiency therefore affects all variants in the production program, not only the initial flight test article. The following variant-specific scope considerations are elevated by this change:

**MAAP-1C (Cargo, Fully Autonomous):** This variant carries the most demanding autonomy certification requirement — zero-pilot autonomous operations as the KPP-07 Objective milestone. It represents the furthest downstream certification commitment and faces the longest cumulative delay exposure. Customer commitments for MAAP-1C configured deliveries should be reviewed immediately for delivery clause implications.

**MAAP-1M (MEDEVAC):** The KPP-07 Threshold for this variant (reduced-crew certification) is now at risk. Any military customer operational planning tied to reduced-crew MEDEVAC IOC dates must be reassessed.

**MAAP-1F (Firefighting):** This variant retains optional pilot capability longer due to regulatory constraints on unpiloted NAS operations, providing a degree of schedule buffer. However, the variant's operational utility — particularly 24/7 autonomous wildfire suppression capability — depends on the same autonomy certification that is currently deficient. CAL FIRE initial deliveries and wildfire season operational commitments are specifically at risk given the seasonal criticality of fire operations (peak season May through October).

**MAAP-1A (Assault):** Formation autonomy applications interface with the green aircraft through ICDs. Depending on the root cause of the certification deficiency, the boundary integrity of these interfaces should be verified as part of the Chief Engineer's scope assessment.

### 2.4 Configuration Control Board Actions Required

The following CCB actions are formally required and shall be initiated within 30 days of this document's effective date:

1. Formal revision of program schedule milestones (First Flight, IOT&E entry, qualification, FRP gate) per Section 3.
2. KPP-07 risk status change documentation and MDA deviation report initiation.
3. RVTM-0001 closure date revision for all autonomy-related requirements.
4. C-1 unit cost constraint achievability assessment under the revised schedule, given FY25-dollar real-value erosion.
5. Scope boundary assessment for autonomy software remediation per Section 2.2.

---

## 3. Milestone List & Schedule Baseline

### 3.1 Revised Milestone Baseline

The following table constitutes the revised program milestone baseline effective upon CCB approval of this update. All dates represent best-case estimates assuming the autonomy software certification issue is fully resolved within the 6-month delay window. A confidence assessment for this assumption is provided in Section 3.3.

| Milestone | Original Baseline Date | Revised Baseline Date | Slip | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomy Software Development Complete (WBS 5.8.2) | 01-Oct-2029 | 01-Apr-2030 (est.) | +6 months | Root cause activity; revised date is an estimate pending recovery plan |
| Software Integration Testing Complete (WBS 5.8.4) | 22-Jan-2030 | 22-Jul-2030 (est.) | +6 months | Directly dependent on WBS 5.8.2 |
| Software V&V Complete (WBS 5.8.5) | 17-Apr-2030 | 17-Oct-2030 (est.) | +6 months | Hard gate for TRR |
| Test Readiness Review (TRR) | 08-Jan-2030 | ~Jul-Aug-2030 | +6 to +7 months | Cannot close until autonomy V&V complete |
| Flight Safety Review | 07-Jan-2030 | ~Jul-Aug-2030 | +6 to +7 months | Concurrent with TRR |
| First Flight (WBS 6.3.2) | 21-May-2030 | ~21-Nov-2030 | **+6 months** | **Critical path — program re-baseline anchor** |
| Flight Test Range Activated | 22-Apr-2030 | ~22-Oct-2030 | +6 months | Contract amendment required |
| Initial Envelope Cleared | 16-Sep-2030 | ~16-Mar-2031 | +6 months | |
| Supersonic Flight Achieved | 24-Feb-2031 | ~24-Aug-2031 | +6 months | |
| High Altitude Capability Demonstrated | 24-Mar-2031 | ~24-Sep-2031 | +6 months | |
| Weapons Integration Complete | 14-Jul-2031 | ~14-Jan-2032 | +6 months | |
| Qualification Complete | 29-Dec-2031 | ~29-Jun-2032 | +6 months | Hard gate for production transition |
| Airworthiness Certified | 05-Jan-2032 | ~05-Jul-2032 | +6 months | |
| Production Transition Start | 06-Jan-2032 | ~06-Jul-2032 | +6 months | |
| First Production Aircraft (PA-1) | 05-Apr-2033 | ~05-Oct-2033 | **+6 months** | Customer notification required |
| LRIP Lot 1 (3 aircraft) | 04-Jul-2034 | ~04-Jan-2035 | **+6 months** | Liquidated damages exposure; see Section 5 |
| FRP Decision Gate | Q3-2030 | ~Q1-2031 | +6 months | Cannot proceed without KPP closure |
| IOC | Q1-2030 | Q3-2030 (minimum) | Re-baseline required | Original date predates revised First Flight; IOC re-baselining is mandatory |

> **Critical Note on IOC:** The baseline IMS documented IOC at Q1-2030, which is prior to the revised First Flight date of November 2030. This internal inconsistency, noted in the pre-change analysis, must be formally resolved through the re-baselining process. IOC is redefined as the earliest operationally meaningful milestone achievable following Airworthiness Certification, now estimated at Q3-2032 at the earliest under a standard production transition timeline.

### 3.2 Critical Path Status

The critical path float remains zero under the revised baseline. No schedule margin has been added by this change — the slip has been absorbed into revised milestone dates without the insertion of additional float. The following critical path chain governs all planning:

> **Autonomy Software V&V → TRR → First Flight → Phase 6A Initial Envelope → Phase 6B Expansion → Phase 6C Performance Validation → Phase 6D Autonomous Systems Validation → Qualification → Production Transition → PA-1 → LRIP Lot 1**

All activities on this chain have zero float. Any further delay at any node propagates fully and immediately to all downstream milestones.

### 3.3 Schedule Confidence Assessment — The Critical Risk of a Second Delay

The 6-month delay estimate carries significant uncertainty. Program leadership must recognize that the estimate of "6 months" to resolve autonomy software certification issues is an internal planning assumption, not a commitment from the FAA or from the verification and validation process. The following factors reduce confidence in a clean 6-month resolution:

**Root Cause Classification:** The recovery timeline differs materially based on the nature of the deficiency. If the issue is a DO-178C process compliance gap (incomplete requirements traceability, MC/DC coverage shortfall, tool qualification deficiency), a 3-to-4-month correction is plausible. If the issue is a functional safety architecture deficiency — meaning the software does not achieve its allocated reliability requirement of ≤1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour — remediation requires requirements analysis, design change, re-implementation, and full reverification of affected requirements, a process that typically requires 6 to 12 months under rigorous DAL A discipline. The root cause has not been formally classified in the program record as of this update's preparation; this classification is Priority 1 for the Chief Software Engineer and Certification Manager.

**Regulatory Timeline Uncertainty:** FAA Special Conditions negotiations for Level 4 autonomous aircraft are inherently unpredictable in duration. The timeline is not fully within the program's control. Regulatory authority concurrence on software certification evidence cannot be compressed by adding internal resources.

**Perfect Storm Proximity:** The program's own financial scenario analysis modeled a 12-month autonomous GNC certification delay scenario at a cost of $95 million. The current 6-month delay is the first step on this trajectory. If the autonomy certification issues are not fully bounded and resolved within the delay window, a second slip would be severely damaging — to program finances, to customer relationships, and to corporate confidence. The probability of this Perfect Storm scenario must be formally re-assessed and updated in the risk register within 30 days.

**Recommendation:** Before issuing revised delivery commitments to customers or revised production schedules to suppliers, the Certification Manager shall conduct an independent certification readiness review of the complete autonomy software stack and deliver a confidence-rated recovery schedule (P50, P70, P90 completion dates) to the Program Director within 45 days. If the P70 completion date exceeds 6 months by more than 60 days, this update shall be immediately revised with a larger slip and customers notified accordingly. It is substantially better to issue one complete, accurate schedule revision than two sequential revisions.

### 3.4 Schedule Recovery Options

The following schedule compression opportunities are available and should be formally evaluated during the 30-to-90-day mitigation window:

**Option A — Optionally-Piloted Configuration First Flight:** If the Phase 6 flight test program can be initiated in a piloted mode with a reduced autonomy envelope, the software certification issue may be contained to the autonomous certification phase rather than delaying First Flight itself. This is the highest-leverage single mitigation available. The PRB references this approach in Section 3.5. The Chief Engineer and Certification Manager shall deliver a feasibility assessment within 30 days.

**Option B — Parallel Flight Test Sequencing:** The 24-month flight test program has some internal sequencing flexibility. Accelerating Phase 6A and 6B envelope expansion activities while autonomous systems validation (Phase 6D) continues in parallel on the ground could recover 2 to 3 months at an estimated cost of $8 million to $15 million, which is substantially less than the liquidated damages exposure of continued delay.

**Option C — Additional Flight Test Article Activation:** Activating a second flight test article to parallelize test phases could recover schedule. This option should be evaluated on a cost-versus-delivery-penalty basis given that each additional test article represents significant capital investment.

**Option D — Additional Autonomy Software Certification Resources:** Assess whether the 120-engineer internal autonomy software team can be surge-resourced with DO-178C DAL A specialists. Note that outsourcing the fix is not recommended — it adds markup costs and reduces IP control — but contractor augmentation with software verification specialists is viable and may accelerate V&V evidence generation.

---

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification and Authority

This change is formally classified as a **Class I Program Change** under PRB Section 12.1, as it materially affects the program cost baseline, the schedule baseline, KPP delivery commitments, and constraint compliance status. Class I changes require:

- CCB action chaired by the Program Manager with the Chief Engineer as technical authority
- MDA notification
- Customer notification per contractual requirements
- Corporate executive escalation per Financial and Contract Management Section 12.2

No activity reflecting the revised schedule baseline shall be communicated to external parties — customers, suppliers, or regulatory agencies — prior to formal CCB approval of this update. All internal planning may proceed against the revised dates in Section 3.

### 4.2 Mandatory Escalation Actions

The following escalation actions are triggered by this change and shall be executed within the timelines specified. These are not discretionary.

| Escalation Action | Authority Level | Timeline | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program VP notification | Program VP | Immediate (within 48 hours of CCB approval) | Schedule variance >30 days on critical path |
| CFO and CEO notification | CFO / CEO | Within 5 business days | Schedule variance >90 days on major milestone; forecast margin <8.5% |
| Board of Directors notification | Board | Within 10 business days | Schedule variance >90 days on major milestone per governance framework |
| Customer notification — schedule impact | Contracts / Legal | Within 15 business days, or per contract notification clauses | Major milestone slip affecting delivery commitments |
| MDA deviation report — KPP-07 | Program Director | Within 15 business days | KPP-07 designated RED; PRB §7.1 requirement |
| Customer notification — liquidated damages assessment | Contracts / Legal | Within 20 business days | LD exposure confirmation; contractual duty |
| ITAR customer notification (export customers) | Export Compliance | Within 10 business days | ITAR licensing terms require State Department coordination on schedule changes affecting ITAR-controlled deliveries |

### 4.3 Change Control Governance During the Recovery Period

Given the critically low management reserve position following absorption of this change (less than $12 million remaining against $74 million initial reserve), the Program Manager is instituting the following enhanced change governance measures effective immediately:

**Enhanced CCB Threshold:** Any subsequent scope, schedule, or cost change with an estimated value exceeding $1 million or a schedule impact exceeding 30 days shall be elevated to CCB within 72 hours of identification, regardless of the normal review cycle schedule.

**Weekly EVM Reporting:** Cost Performance Index and Schedule Performance Index shall be reported weekly to the Program Director until CPI and SPI are both above 0.95 for four consecutive weeks following the revised baseline establishment.

**30-Day Recovery Plan Checkpoint:** A formal program recovery plan shall be presented to the Program Director, CFO, and CEO within 30 days of this update's effective date. The recovery plan shall include: root cause classification, P50/P70/P90 recovery schedule, funded mitigation actions, reserve replenishment request, and customer communication status.

**60-Day External Review:** Given the magnitude of this change and the near-exhaustion of management reserve, the Program Manager shall engage corporate leadership to commission an independent program assessment at day 60. This assessment shall evaluate the adequacy of the recovery plan, the realism of the revised schedule, and the status of corporate contingency funding commitment.

### 4.4 Equitable Adjustment Assessment

Contracts and Legal shall immediately open a formal Equitable Adjustment documentation file. The following questions shall be assessed and documented within 20 business days:

1. Did the autonomy certification requirements evolve post-contract due to regulatory standard changes imposed by the FAA (Special Conditions scope expansion)?
2. Were any certification requirements imposed by customer-specified interpretive guidance that was not contractually defined at contract award?
3. Are there any customer-caused dimensions to the certification delay (late requirements, delayed government-furnished data or approvals)?

If any portion of the delay is determined to have a customer-caused dimension, an EA claim shall be formally initiated. The program recovered 66% of customer-caused impacts ($11 million of $18.3 million) on a prior comparable program through proactive EA pursuit. This discipline shall be applied here.

---

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Revised Cost Baseline

The program cost baseline is revised to incorporate the direct financial impact of the 6-month First Flight delay. The following table presents the revised cost baseline, consistent with the pre-quantified financial scenario documented in the Financial and Contract Management plan Section 3.2.

| Cost Category | Original Baseline | Incremental Impact | Revised Baseline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extended Engineering Labor (6 months, 350 peak staff) | — | +$18M – $25M | Added to EAC |
| Autonomy Software Remediation (DO-178C DAL A re-verification) | — | +$8M – $15M | Added to EAC |
| Facility and Test Infrastructure Holding Costs | — | +$4M – $6M | Added to EAC |
| Supplier Re-sequencing and Renegotiation | — | +$3M – $6M | Added to EAC |
| FTA-1 Storage, Maintenance, and Readiness Preservation | — | +$3M – $6M | Added to EAC |
| Liquidated Damages (Revenue Reduction) | — | -$8M | Revenue reduction |
| **Direct Cost Impact (Base Scenario)** | | **+$62M (base) / +$78M–$95M (realistic)** | |
| **Total Financial Exposure (including LD)** | | **~$70M (base) / ~$86M–$103M (realistic)** | |

The base scenario of +$62M and -$8M in liquidated damages was pre-quantified in the program's own financial modeling and represents the floor of financial exposure. The realistic total exposure range of $78 million to $95 million reflects additional cost drivers not fully captured in the base scenario: additional DO-178C compliance work and regulatory engagement ($8M–$15M), test range and chase aircraft contract re-sequencing penalties ($2M–$4M), and key flight test personnel retention costs during the delay period ($3M–$8M).

### 5.2 Non-Recurring Cost Impact

The Bill of Materials documents total non-recurring costs of $793.8 million. The 6-month delay specifically impacts the following NRC line items:

| NRC Line Item | Baseline | Estimated Increment | Revised Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engineering Development | $450M | +$20M – $40M | $470M – $490M |
| Flight Test Program | $185M | +$15M – $30M | $200M – $215M |
| Certification and Qualification | $95M | +$10M – $20M | $105M – $115M |
| **Total NRC** | **$793.8M** | **+$45M – $90M** | **~$839M – $884M** |

This NRC increase revises the per-aircraft amortized non-recurring cost from the baseline of $7,938,150 to a range of $8,388,150 (low overrun) to $8,838,150 (high overrun) per aircraft across the 100-unit amortization base. This increase propagates to all five variant program unit costs and will be reflected in the next BOM revision.

### 5.3 Management Reserve Status

This is the most acute financial governance issue generated by this change. The program's management reserve position is critically compromised:

| Reserve Metric | Status |
|---|---|
| Original management reserve | $74M (1.5% of contract value) |
| Reserve consumed by this change (base scenario) | $62M (84% of total reserve) |
| Remaining reserve (post-change, base scenario) | ~$12M |
| Recommended P70 reserve (program's own lessons-learned benchmark) | $125M |
| Reserve adequacy trigger (20% remaining with >50% program remaining) | Breached |

With less than $12 million in management reserve remaining against a multi-billion-dollar development and production program with known open risks, the program is in a reserve adequacy crisis. A formal Reserve Replenishment Plan shall be submitted to the CFO within 30 days of this update's effective date. The minimum replenishment target is $50 million to restore P50 coverage; the recommended replenishment target to restore P70 coverage is $125 million minus the post-change remaining balance, approximately $113 million in total reserve commitment.

### 5.4 Margin Impact and Escalation Status

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Baseline program margin | 9.9% (target) |
| Post-change margin (base scenario, -147 bps) | ~8.4% |
| Mandatory escalation threshold | 8.5% |
| Escalation status | **TRIGGERED** |

The base-case margin of approximately 8.4% falls below the 8.5% mandatory escalation threshold defined in the Financial and Contract Management governance framework. This triggers mandatory escalation from Program VP to CFO to CEO per the framework. If any additional cost growth materializes — through learning curve underperformance, supplier cost reduction failures, overhead rate growth, or an extension of the certification delay beyond 6 months — margin will deteriorate further and potentially approach breakeven under the documented compound risk scenarios.

### 5.5 Cash Flow Implications

The delay creates a material cash flow trough. The specific drivers are:

**Extended Engineering Burn Rate:** 350 peak engineers at loaded rates continuing for 6 additional months without corresponding milestone payment triggers represents an estimated $25 million to $35 million in additional cash outflow before revenue events are reached.

**Milestone Payment Deferral:** If First Flight is a contract milestone payment trigger, the 6-month slip defers that cash inflow. The program has previously experienced DSO peaks of 58 days during development transition periods, requiring corporate credit line draws. The Program Finance Director shall model the specific cash flow impact and coordinate with Treasury on working capital adequacy.

**Capital Investment Carrying Cost:** The $235 million Phase 1 capital deployment is mid-execution. Deployment of capital against a schedule that has slipped creates carrying costs without corresponding production activity. The 2025 capital spend profile of $68 million will partially shift into 2026, creating compression against the $52 million LRIP-2 preparation budget planned for that year.

### 5.6 Program Unit Cost Constraint (C-1) Risk Assessment

The PRB constraint C-1 requires that the program not exceed $21.5 million per aircraft in FY25 dollars at FRP Lot 5 pricing. This constraint carries no inflation escalation clause as written. A 6-month delay introduces two threats to C-1 compliance: direct NRC cost growth that elevates the amortized cost per aircraft, and FRP timeline slippage that reduces the real purchasing power of the FY25-dollar cap in then-year execution. The Chief Financial Officer and Contracts Director shall formally assess C-1 achievability under the revised schedule and report findings to the CCB within 60 days.

---

## 6. Risk Management Plan & Risk Register

### 6.1 Risk Register Status Update

The program risk register, as it existed under the January 2025 baseline, requires immediate comprehensive revision. Multiple risks coded as "Open/Anticipated" have now materialized or been directly elevated. The risk register in its current state understates the program's actual risk posture, and this misrepresentation must be corrected before the next Program Management Review.

**NEW-RISK-04: Competitive Disadvantage from Schedule Slip (Medium-High — Score 10)**
MAAP-1 is positioned as the first-in-class autonomous heavy-lift helicopter. A 6-month delay provides competitors additional time to close the technology gap and potentially erodes the first-mover advantage underpinning the 450+ aircraft market demand forecast. Owner: Program Director / Business Development Director. Mitigation: Maintain customer communication posture emphasizing certification diligence as a quality differentiator; protect LRIP slot commitments.

**NEW-RISK-05: Supplier Capacity Reallocation During Delay (Medium-High — Score 12)**
Sole-source suppliers (engines, transmission, rotor blades, landing gear) have committed capacity and resources to the MAAP-1 schedule. A 6-month slip may cause suppliers to reallocate production capacity to other customers. When MAAP-1 is ready to resume, supplier capacity may not be available on the revised schedule, creating a secondary delay that extends program impact beyond 6 months. Owner: Supply Chain Director. Mitigation: Immediate supplier notification with capacity reservation agreements; WIP holdings negotiations; consider partial advance payments to hold critical slots.

**NEW-RISK-06: DO-178C Findings Migration to Production Configuration (High — Score 12)**
If the autonomy software certification issues are not definitively and completely resolved before First Flight, the deficiency may migrate into the production configuration. At production scale, every delivered aircraft must pass the pre-delivery Autonomous Systems Check. A systematic autonomy software deficiency that generates production nonconformances would halt deliveries at scale, compounding the cost and schedule impact by orders of magnitude. Owner: Chief Software Engineer / Quality Director. Mitigation: Do not authorize production configuration release of autonomy software build until full certification closure is achieved; maintain separation between development/test builds and production baseline.

The TEMP identifies Phase 7 (Swarm and Multi-Aircraft Testing) as overlapping with Phase 6, using FTA-4, FTA-5, and FTA-6. A 6-month slip to First Flight risks compressing or eliminating this overlap, converting parallel activities to sequential ones and adding schedule risk beyond the first-order 6-month slip. Multi-aircraft autonomous coordination is the program's most novel and least-precedented test activity. Owner: Test and Evaluation Director. Mitigation: Review Phase 7 activation criteria and assess whether FTA-4/5/6 preparations can be accelerated to preserve overlap.
